Kinematics.skill: KIN-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.1) Kinematics - 1.1 Position, Velocity, and Acceleration
Kinematics.prompt: This topic covers position, velocity, and acceleration for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: KIN-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students should understand that velocity is defined as the time derivative of position, v(t) = dx/dt, and acceleration is the time derivative of velocity, a(t) = dv/dt = d^2x/dt^2. Conversely, position is found by integrating velocity: x(t) = integral of v(t) dt, and velocity by integrating acceleration: v(t) = integral of a(t) dt. Instantaneous quantities are obtained through limits and derivatives, unlike the algebraic averages used in non-calculus courses. Students must apply differential and integral calculus to analyze motion with both constant and variable acceleration. The chain rule yields a = v(dv/dx), a powerful relation for position-dependent acceleration. Connect to Newton's second law and energy methods. AP exam note: free-response problems frequently require taking derivatives or evaluating definite integrals of motion functions. Vocabulary: position, velocity, acceleration, derivative, integral, instantaneous, chain rule, kinematics.

Kinematics.skill: KIN-1.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.2) Kinematics - 1.2 Representations of Motion
Kinematics.prompt: This topic covers representations of motion for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: KIN-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Students should understand how to interpret and translate between position-time, velocity-time, and acceleration-time graphs using calculus. The slope of x(t) at any point gives the instantaneous velocity (dx/dt), and the slope of v(t) gives instantaneous acceleration (dv/dt). The area under a v(t) curve over an interval gives displacement (definite integral of v dt), and the area under an a(t) curve gives change in velocity. Students must sketch derivative and antiderivative graphs from given motion graphs and evaluate definite integrals graphically. Motion diagrams and parametric descriptions of trajectories are also essential tools. Connect to experimental data analysis and curve fitting of kinematic functions. AP exam note: graphical calculus questions—identifying slopes as derivatives and areas as integrals—appear on both multiple-choice and free-response sections. Vocabulary: slope, area under curve, definite integral, tangent line, concavity, inflection point, motion diagram, parametric.

Kinematics.skill: KIN-2.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.3) Kinematics - 1.3 Equations of Motion for Constant Acceleration
Kinematics.prompt: This topic covers equations of motion for constant acceleration for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: KIN-2.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Students should understand that when acceleration is constant, integrating a = constant once gives v(t) = v_0 + at, and integrating again gives x(t) = x_0 + v_0*t + (1/2)at^2. The time-independent equation v^2 = v_0^2 + 2a(x - x_0) is derived by eliminating time between these two results. Students must derive these kinematic equations through integration and apply them to solve problems including free fall (a = -g) and motion along inclined planes. The calculus derivation reinforces that these equations are only valid when acceleration does not vary with time. Connect to projectile motion, Newton's second law, and work-energy methods for non-constant acceleration cases. AP exam note: deriving kinematic equations from integration is a testable skill; students should also know when these equations do not apply. Vocabulary: constant acceleration, integration, free fall, initial conditions, kinematic equations, time-independent equation, gravitational acceleration.

Kinematics.skill: KIN-2.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.4) Kinematics - 1.4 Motion in Two Dimensions
Kinematics.prompt: This topic covers motion in two dimensions for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: KIN-2.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Students should understand that two-dimensional motion is analyzed by treating the x- and y-components independently, each governed by its own differential equation. For projectile motion with constant gravitational acceleration, x(t) = x_0 + v_{0x}t and y(t) = y_0 + v_{0y}t - (1/2)gt^2 are obtained by integrating the component accelerations. Vector position, velocity, and acceleration are expressed using unit vectors: r(t) = x(t)i + y(t)j, with v = dr/dt and a = dv/dt applied component-wise. Students must resolve initial velocity vectors, derive trajectory equations by eliminating time, and solve for range and maximum height. Parametric equations describe the path, and the velocity vector is always tangent to the trajectory. Connect to relative motion, circular motion, and non-uniform acceleration in 2D. AP exam note: projectile motion derivations and vector calculus in two dimensions are commonly tested. Vocabulary: projectile motion, component, unit vector, trajectory, parametric equations, range, tangent vector, vector function.

Newton's Laws of Motion.skill: FRC-1.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.1) Newton's Laws of Motion - 2.1 Newton's First Law
Newton's Laws of Motion.prompt: This topic covers Newton's First Law for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: FRC-1.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). Students should understand that an object remains at rest or moves with constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force. This law establishes the concept of inertial reference frames—frames in which Newton's laws hold without fictitious forces. Mass quantifies inertia, the resistance to changes in motion. Mathematically, when the vector sum of all forces is zero (F_net = 0), dv/dt = 0, meaning velocity is constant. Students must identify equilibrium conditions both for objects at rest (static equilibrium) and objects moving at constant velocity (dynamic equilibrium). Free-body diagrams are the essential tool for systematically cataloging all forces. Connect to Newton's second law, non-inertial frames and pseudo-forces, and translational equilibrium analysis. AP exam note: distinguishing inertial from non-inertial frames and correctly identifying zero-net-force conditions are key conceptual skills. Vocabulary: inertia, inertial reference frame, equilibrium, net force, free-body diagram, static equilibrium, dynamic equilibrium, mass.

Newton's Laws of Motion.skill: FRC-1.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.2) Newton's Laws of Motion - 2.2 Newton's Second Law
Newton's Laws of Motion.prompt: This topic covers Newton's Second Law for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: FRC-1.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Students should understand that the net force on an object equals the product of its mass and acceleration: F_net = ma, or equivalently F_net = dp/dt where p = mv is the linear momentum. This is a vector differential equation that governs translational motion. When mass is constant, F_net = m(dv/dt) = m(d^2x/dt^2), a second-order ordinary differential equation. Students must set up and solve differential equations of motion for systems with constant and variable forces, including cases where force depends on time, velocity, or position (e.g., drag forces F = -bv, or spring forces F = -kx). Component equations along chosen axes must be written independently. Connect to kinematics, impulse-momentum, and work-energy approaches. AP exam note: setting up and solving the differential equation F = ma for various force functions is a core calculus-based skill. Vocabulary: net force, acceleration, differential equation, momentum, second-order ODE, component, variable force, drag.

Newton's Laws of Motion.skill: FRC-1.C (Unit 2, Topic 2.3) Newton's Laws of Motion - 2.3 Newton's Third Law
Newton's Laws of Motion.prompt: This topic covers Newton's Third Law for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: FRC-1.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students should understand that if object A exerts a force on object B, then object B simultaneously exerts a force on A that is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction: F_AB = -F_BA. These action-reaction pairs are always the same type of force, act on different objects, and exist simultaneously. They never cancel when analyzing a single object because they act on different bodies. When analyzing a system of interacting objects, internal third-law pairs cancel, and only external forces affect the system's center-of-mass acceleration (F_ext = Ma_cm). Students must identify third-law pairs in multi-body problems, correctly draw separate free-body diagrams for each object, and apply constraints (e.g., same magnitude of acceleration for connected objects). Connect to momentum conservation, collisions, and system-level analysis. AP exam note: multi-body problems requiring separate free-body diagrams and constraint equations are standard. Vocabulary: action-reaction pair, third law, internal force, external force, constraint, system, interaction, contact force.

Newton's Laws of Motion.skill: FRC-2.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.4) Newton's Laws of Motion - 2.4 Applications of Newton's Laws
Newton's Laws of Motion.prompt: This topic covers applications of Newton's Laws for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: FRC-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students should understand how to apply F_net = ma to complex scenarios including inclined planes, Atwood machines, pulley systems, and connected objects with ropes and strings. For each object, draw a free-body diagram, choose a coordinate system (often tilted to align with motion), write component equations, and solve the resulting system of equations. Constraint conditions—such as the string length remaining constant or objects sharing the same acceleration magnitude—reduce the number of unknowns. For velocity-dependent forces like linear drag (F = -bv), the equation m(dv/dt) = F_applied - bv is a first-order linear ODE solved by separation of variables to find v(t) and the terminal velocity v_terminal = F_applied/b. Students must solve both algebraically and using calculus techniques. Connect to energy methods and rotational dynamics. AP exam note: multi-step force problems requiring differential equation solutions are signature Physics C questions. Vocabulary: inclined plane, Atwood machine, constraint, tension, normal force, terminal velocity, separation of variables, ODE.

Newton's Laws of Motion.skill: FRC-2.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.5) Newton's Laws of Motion - 2.5 Friction
Newton's Laws of Motion.prompt: This topic covers friction for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: FRC-2.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). Students should understand that friction is a contact force opposing relative motion or the tendency of relative motion between surfaces. Static friction adjusts up to a maximum value: f_s <= mu_s * N, where mu_s is the coefficient of static friction and N is the normal force. Kinetic friction is constant: f_k = mu_k * N, with mu_k typically less than mu_s. The normal force must be calculated from Newton's second law in the perpendicular direction—it is not always equal to mg (e.g., on inclines, or with applied forces at angles). Students must incorporate friction into F = ma equations and solve the resulting differential or algebraic equations. For objects on inclines, friction components are parallel to the surface. The transition from static to kinetic friction determines the threshold for motion onset. Connect to work done by friction, energy dissipation, and circular motion on banked curves. AP exam note: friction on inclined planes and in multi-body systems is frequently tested. Vocabulary: static friction, kinetic friction, coefficient of friction, normal force, threshold, incline, energy dissipation.

Newton's Laws of Motion.skill: FRC-3.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.6) Newton's Laws of Motion - 2.6 Circular Motion
Newton's Laws of Motion.prompt: This topic covers circular motion for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: FRC-3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Students should understand that uniform circular motion requires a centripetal acceleration a_c = v^2/r = omega^2 * r directed toward the center, produced by a net centripetal force F_c = mv^2/r. This is not a new force but the resultant of real forces (gravity, tension, normal force, friction). In non-uniform circular motion, there is also a tangential acceleration a_t = dv/dt = r * d(omega)/dt, so the net acceleration has both radial and tangential components: a = sqrt(a_c^2 + a_t^2). Angular quantities are related by theta, omega = d(theta)/dt, and alpha = d(omega)/dt. Students must analyze vertical circles, banked curves (with and without friction), and conical pendulums by applying Newton's second law in radial and tangential directions. The critical condition at the top of a vertical loop (minimum speed) is found by setting N = 0. Connect to gravitation, orbital mechanics, and rotational kinematics. AP exam note: circular motion problems requiring calculus for non-uniform cases are common. Vocabulary: centripetal, tangential, angular velocity, angular acceleration, radial, banked curve, vertical circle, conical pendulum.

Work, Energy, and Power.skill: ENE-1.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.1) Work, Energy, and Power - 3.1 Work and Work-Energy Theorem
Work, Energy, and Power.prompt: This topic covers work and the work-energy theorem for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: ENE-1.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that work done by a force is defined as the line integral W = integral of F dot dr along the path of motion. For a constant force, this reduces to W = F*d*cos(theta). The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: W_net = Delta(KE) = (1/2)mv_f^2 - (1/2)mv_i^2. For variable forces, students must evaluate the integral W = integral from x_i to x_f of F(x) dx, which graphically represents the area under the force-displacement curve. Work is a scalar quantity and can be positive (force component along motion), negative (force component opposing motion), or zero (force perpendicular to motion). Students must compute work using dot products and definite integrals. Connect to potential energy, conservation of energy, and power. AP exam note: evaluating work integrals for position-dependent forces is a key calculus-based skill. Vocabulary: work, kinetic energy, work-energy theorem, line integral, dot product, scalar, variable force, displacement.

Work, Energy, and Power.skill: ENE-2.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.2) Work, Energy, and Power - 3.2 Forces and Potential Energy
Work, Energy, and Power.prompt: This topic covers forces and potential energy for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: ENE-2.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand that a conservative force is one for which the work done is path-independent and depends only on initial and final positions. For conservative forces, a potential energy function U(x) can be defined such that F(x) = -dU/dx (force is the negative derivative of potential energy). Conversely, Delta(U) = -integral of F dx. Gravitational potential energy near Earth's surface is U = mgy; elastic potential energy for a spring is U = (1/2)kx^2. Students must derive force from a given potential energy function and vice versa. Potential energy diagrams (U vs. x graphs) reveal equilibrium positions: stable equilibrium at U minima (d^2U/dx^2 > 0), unstable equilibrium at U maxima (d^2U/dx^2 < 0). Turning points occur where total energy E = U(x). Non-conservative forces (friction, drag) have no associated potential energy. Connect to conservation of energy and oscillatory motion near equilibrium. AP exam note: deriving F from U(x) and interpreting energy diagrams are signature Physics C skills. Vocabulary: conservative force, potential energy, potential energy diagram, stable equilibrium, unstable equilibrium, turning point, spring constant.

Work, Energy, and Power.skill: ENE-3.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.3) Work, Energy, and Power - 3.3 Conservation of Energy
Work, Energy, and Power.prompt: This topic covers conservation of energy for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: ENE-3.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand that when only conservative forces act, the total mechanical energy E = KE + U is conserved: (1/2)mv_i^2 + U_i = (1/2)mv_f^2 + U_f. When non-conservative forces (friction, drag) are present, the work-energy theorem gives W_nc = Delta(KE) + Delta(U) = Delta(E), meaning non-conservative work equals the change in total mechanical energy. Energy methods are often more efficient than force methods for problems involving variable forces or complex paths. Students must apply energy conservation to systems with gravitational and elastic potential energy, calculate energy lost to friction, and analyze motion using potential energy diagrams. Differentiating E = (1/2)mv^2 + U(x) with respect to time and setting dE/dt = 0 yields F = ma, demonstrating the equivalence of energy and force approaches. Connect to oscillations, orbital energy, and thermodynamic energy concepts. AP exam note: energy conservation problems requiring integration or differentiation are standard. Vocabulary: mechanical energy, conservation of energy, non-conservative force, dissipation, total energy, energy diagram, work-energy.

Work, Energy, and Power.skill: ENE-4.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.4) Work, Energy, and Power - 3.4 Power
Work, Energy, and Power.prompt: This topic covers power for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: ENE-4.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand that power is the time rate of doing work or transferring energy: P = dW/dt. For a force acting on a moving object, the instantaneous power is P = F dot v (the dot product of the force and velocity vectors). Average power is P_avg = Delta(W)/Delta(t) = Delta(E)/Delta(t). Power is measured in watts (1 W = 1 J/s). For cases where force or velocity varies with time, students must express P as a function of time using calculus and, if needed, integrate power to find total work: W = integral of P dt. In problems involving constant power output (e.g., an engine), the equation P = Fv combined with F = ma yields differential equations that describe the acceleration and velocity as functions of time. At terminal velocity under constant power, all power goes to overcoming drag: P = F_drag * v_terminal. Connect to energy conservation, efficiency, and mechanical systems. AP exam note: problems combining power with Newton's second law and requiring differential equation solutions are common. Vocabulary: power, watt, instantaneous power, average power, dot product, efficiency, energy transfer rate, terminal velocity.

Systems of Particles and Linear Momentum.skill: SYS-1.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.1) Systems of Particles and Linear Momentum - 4.1 Center of Mass
Systems of Particles and Linear Momentum.prompt: This topic covers center of mass for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: SYS-1.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). Students should understand that the center of mass (COM) of a system of particles is defined as r_cm = (1/M) * sum(m_i * r_i), where M is the total mass. For continuous mass distributions, this becomes r_cm = (1/M) * integral of r dm, requiring integration over the body using appropriate mass elements (dm = lambda dx for linear, dm = sigma dA for surface, dm = rho dV for volume distributions). The velocity of the center of mass is v_cm = (1/M) * sum(m_i * v_i) = dp_total/M, and the acceleration is a_cm = F_ext/M, showing that external forces alone govern COM motion. Internal forces cancel in pairs by Newton's third law. Students must calculate COM positions using both summation and integration, and analyze COM motion for multi-body systems. Connect to momentum conservation, collisions, and rotational dynamics. AP exam note: integration to find center of mass of rods and other simple shapes is a frequently tested calculus skill. Vocabulary: center of mass, mass distribution, linear density, surface density, volume density, internal force, external force, integration.

Systems of Particles and Linear Momentum.skill: MOM-1.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.2) Systems of Particles and Linear Momentum - 4.2 Impulse and Momentum
Systems of Particles and Linear Momentum.prompt: This topic covers impulse and momentum for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: MOM-1.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). Students should understand that linear momentum is defined as p = mv (a vector quantity), and Newton's second law in its most general form is F_net = dp/dt. The impulse-momentum theorem states that impulse J = integral of F dt = Delta(p), where the integral is evaluated over the time interval of the force application. For a constant force, J = F * Delta(t). For variable forces, students must evaluate the time integral of F(t) to find impulse, which graphically equals the area under the F(t) curve. Impulse is a vector and changes momentum in the direction of the applied force. Students must analyze collisions and impacts using impulse-momentum, calculate average forces during collisions, and relate the shape of F(t) to momentum change. Connect to conservation of momentum, collisions, and center of mass motion. AP exam note: integrating force-time functions and relating impulse to momentum change are core calculus skills. Vocabulary: momentum, impulse, impulse-momentum theorem, force-time graph, average force, vector, collision, time integral.

Systems of Particles and Linear Momentum.skill: MOM-2.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.3) Systems of Particles and Linear Momentum - 4.3 Conservation of Linear Momentum and Collisions
Systems of Particles and Linear Momentum.prompt: This topic covers conservation of linear momentum and collisions for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: MOM-2.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students should understand that when the net external force on a system is zero, the total momentum is conserved: dp_total/dt = 0, so p_total = constant. This principle applies to all types of collisions. In perfectly elastic collisions, both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved, yielding two equations for two unknowns. In perfectly inelastic collisions, objects stick together (maximum KE loss). In general inelastic collisions, momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not. Students must solve collision problems in one and two dimensions, calculate kinetic energy lost, and determine collision type from given data. The coefficient of restitution e = (v_2f - v_1f)/(v_1i - v_2i) characterizes collision elasticity (e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic). In 2D collisions, momentum conservation applies independently to each component. Connect to center of mass frame analysis, impulse, and energy. AP exam note: 2D collision problems and energy loss calculations are standard. Vocabulary: conservation of momentum, elastic collision, inelastic collision, perfectly inelastic, coefficient of restitution, kinetic energy loss, two-dimensional collision.

Rotation.skill: ROT-1.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.1) Rotation - 5.1 Torque and Rotational Statics
Rotation.prompt: This topic covers torque and rotational statics for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: ROT-1.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.1). Students should understand that torque is defined as the cross product tau = r x F, with magnitude tau = rF*sin(theta), where r is the position vector from the pivot to the point of force application and theta is the angle between r and F. Equivalently, tau = r_perp * F = r * F_perp, where r_perp is the moment arm (lever arm). Torque is a vector quantity whose direction is determined by the right-hand rule. For rotational static equilibrium, both the net force and the net torque about any point must be zero: sum(F) = 0 and sum(tau) = 0. The choice of pivot point is arbitrary for equilibrium problems, and a strategic choice can eliminate unknown forces from the torque equation. Students must solve problems involving beams, ladders, and other rigid bodies in static equilibrium. Connect to rotational dynamics and angular momentum. AP exam note: choosing a convenient pivot to simplify torque calculations is a critical problem-solving strategy. Vocabulary: torque, cross product, moment arm, lever arm, right-hand rule, static equilibrium, pivot, rigid body.

This topic covers rotational kinematics for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: ROT-2.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.2). Students should understand the angular analogues of translational kinematics: angular position theta, angular velocity omega = d(theta)/dt, and angular acceleration alpha = d(omega)/dt = d^2(theta)/dt^2. For constant angular acceleration, the kinematic equations are: omega = omega_0 + alpha*t, theta = theta_0 + omega_0*t + (1/2)alpha*t^2, and omega^2 = omega_0^2 + 2*alpha*(theta - theta_0), derived by direct integration. For variable angular acceleration, students must integrate alpha(t) to find omega(t) and theta(t). The relationships between translational and angular quantities for a point on a rotating body are: s = r*theta, v = r*omega, a_t = r*alpha, and a_c = r*omega^2. Rolling without slipping imposes the constraint v_cm = r*omega. Students must solve problems involving spinning and rolling objects using both differential and integral calculus. Connect to torque, rotational dynamics, and angular momentum. AP exam note: deriving angular quantities through integration and applying rolling constraints are key skills. Vocabulary: angular position, angular velocity, angular acceleration, radian, rolling without slipping, tangential acceleration, centripetal acceleration.

Rotation.skill: ROT-3.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.3) Rotation - 5.3 Rotational Dynamics and Energy
Rotation.prompt: This topic covers rotational dynamics and energy for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: ROT-3.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.3). Students should understand that the rotational analogue of Newton's second law is tau_net = I*alpha, where I is the moment of inertia. The moment of inertia for a system of particles is I = sum(m_i * r_i^2), and for continuous bodies I = integral of r^2 dm, requiring integration with appropriate mass elements. Common results include I = (1/2)MR^2 (solid cylinder), I = MR^2 (hoop), I = (2/5)MR^2 (solid sphere), and I = (1/12)ML^2 (rod about center). The parallel axis theorem I = I_cm + Md^2 shifts the axis of rotation. Rotational kinetic energy is KE_rot = (1/2)I*omega^2. For rolling without slipping, total KE = (1/2)Mv_cm^2 + (1/2)I_cm*omega^2. Students must derive moments of inertia by integration, apply tau = I*alpha to solve dynamics problems, and use energy conservation including rotational energy. Connect to angular momentum and work-energy methods. AP exam note: deriving I by integration and solving combined translational-rotational problems are signature Physics C skills. Vocabulary: moment of inertia, parallel axis theorem, rotational kinetic energy, rolling, torque, angular acceleration, mass element, integration.

Rotation.skill: ROT-4.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.4) Rotation - 5.4 Angular Momentum and Its Conservation
Rotation.prompt: This topic covers angular momentum and its conservation for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: ROT-4.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.4). Students should understand that angular momentum is defined as L = r x p = r x mv for a particle and L = I*omega for a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis. The rotational analogue of Newton's second law in momentum form is tau_net = dL/dt. When the net external torque on a system is zero, angular momentum is conserved: dL/dt = 0, so L = constant. This applies to systems where the moment of inertia changes (e.g., a figure skater pulling in arms: I_i * omega_i = I_f * omega_f). For a particle moving in a straight line, L about a point is L = mvr*sin(theta) = mv*r_perp, which remains constant if no torque acts. Students must calculate angular momentum using cross products, apply conservation of angular momentum to collisions between rotating and translating objects, and analyze systems where I changes. Connect to orbital mechanics, precession, and rotational energy changes. AP exam note: angular momentum conservation in collision and variable-I problems is heavily tested. Vocabulary: angular momentum, cross product, conservation of angular momentum, moment of inertia, torque, rotational collision, right-hand rule.

Oscillations.skill: OSC-1.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.1) Oscillations - 6.1 Simple Harmonic Motion, Springs, and Pendulums
Oscillations.prompt: This topic covers simple harmonic motion, springs, and pendulums for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: OSC-1.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand that simple harmonic motion (SHM) occurs when a restoring force is proportional to displacement: F = -kx for springs. Applying Newton's second law gives the differential equation m(d^2x/dt^2) = -kx, or d^2x/dt^2 + omega^2 * x = 0, where omega = sqrt(k/m). The general solution is x(t) = A*cos(omega*t + phi), with velocity v(t) = -A*omega*sin(omega*t + phi) and acceleration a(t) = -A*omega^2*cos(omega*t + phi). The period is T = 2*pi/omega = 2*pi*sqrt(m/k). For a simple pendulum with small angles, the restoring torque gives d^2(theta)/dt^2 = -(g/L)*theta, yielding T = 2*pi*sqrt(L/g). Physical (compound) pendulums have T = 2*pi*sqrt(I/(mgd)). Students must derive the differential equation, verify solutions, and determine amplitude and phase from initial conditions. Connect to energy in SHM and damped oscillations. AP exam note: deriving and solving the SHM differential equation is a core Physics C skill. Vocabulary: simple harmonic motion, angular frequency, amplitude, phase constant, period, restoring force, differential equation, simple pendulum, physical pendulum.

Oscillations.skill: OSC-2.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.2) Oscillations - 6.2 Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion
Oscillations.prompt: This topic covers energy in simple harmonic motion for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: OSC-2.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that in SHM, energy oscillates between kinetic and potential forms while total mechanical energy remains constant (for undamped systems). For a mass-spring system: KE = (1/2)mv^2 = (1/2)kA^2*sin^2(omega*t + phi) and U = (1/2)kx^2 = (1/2)kA^2*cos^2(omega*t + phi), so E_total = (1/2)kA^2 = constant. Maximum KE occurs at equilibrium (x = 0) and maximum PE occurs at the turning points (x = ±A). The velocity at any position is v = omega*sqrt(A^2 - x^2), derived from energy conservation. Differentiating E_total with respect to time and setting dE/dt = 0 recovers the equation of motion, demonstrating the equivalence of energy and force approaches. For pendulums, gravitational PE replaces elastic PE. Students must graph KE, PE, and total energy as functions of time and position. Connect to potential energy diagrams, damping, and forced oscillations. AP exam note: energy analysis of oscillators and deriving velocity from energy conservation are commonly tested. Vocabulary: kinetic energy, potential energy, total mechanical energy, amplitude, turning point, equilibrium, energy conservation, damping.

Gravitation.prompt: This topic covers gravitational forces for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: GRV-1.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.1). Students should understand Newton's law of universal gravitation: F = -GMm/r^2 (attractive, directed along the line connecting the masses). The gravitational field is g(r) = -GM/r^2 (directed radially inward), giving the force per unit mass at distance r from the center of a spherical mass M. The shell theorem states that a uniform spherical shell attracts an external particle as if all mass were concentrated at its center, and exerts zero force on a particle inside it. For continuous mass distributions, the gravitational force is found by integrating dF = -G dm/r^2 over the distribution. The gravitational potential energy is U(r) = -GMm/r (with U = 0 at infinity), derived by integrating the force: U = -integral from infinity to r of F dr. Students must derive gravitational PE from the force law and solve problems involving superposition of gravitational forces. Connect to orbital mechanics, escape velocity, and energy conservation. AP exam note: integration to derive U(r) and applying the shell theorem are key calculus-based skills. Vocabulary: universal gravitation, gravitational field, shell theorem, gravitational potential energy, superposition, radial, inverse-square law.

Gravitation.skill: GRV-2.A (Unit 7, Topic 7.2) Gravitation - 7.2 Orbits of Planets and Satellites
Gravitation.prompt: This topic covers orbits of planets and satellites for AP Physics C: Mechanics students (CED: GRV-2.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.2). Students should understand that for a circular orbit, the gravitational force provides centripetal acceleration: GMm/r^2 = mv^2/r, giving orbital speed v = sqrt(GM/r) and period T = 2*pi*r^(3/2)/sqrt(GM), which is Kepler's third law (T^2 proportional to r^3). The total energy of an orbiting object is E = KE + U = (1/2)mv^2 - GMm/r = -GMm/(2r), which is negative for bound orbits. Escape velocity is found by setting E = 0: v_escape = sqrt(2GM/r). For elliptical orbits, the semi-major axis a replaces r in the energy and period formulas: E = -GMm/(2a) and T^2 = (4*pi^2/GM)*a^3. Angular momentum is conserved in all orbits (central force has zero torque), so L = mvr for circular orbits and mv_1*r_1 = mv_2*r_2 at different points in elliptical orbits. Students must derive orbital parameters, calculate escape velocity, and analyze energy and momentum in orbital problems. Connect to gravitational PE, conservation laws, and Kepler's laws. AP exam note: deriving orbital velocity, period, and escape speed from first principles is expected. Vocabulary: orbital velocity, escape velocity, Kepler's laws, semi-major axis, bound orbit, gravitational potential energy, angular momentum, central force.
